elements.sent-2411-165: For both instance classes, Gurobi's performance in obtaining dual bounds is significantly worse as it does not exploit decomposability.
elements.sent-2411-166: We show that constructing Lagrangian duals which achieve the twin goal of zero duality gap and maintaining decomposability of the sub-problems, comes at the cost of solving more challenging sub-problems with non-linear objective functions.
elements.sent-2411-167: Thus, this approach is perhaps most suitable when we have a large number of sub-problems, but each subproblem is not too large.
elements.sent-2411-168: An open question is whether one can achieve Lagrangian duals with decomposability and zero duality gap while solving an "easier" subproblem in each iteration.
elements.sent-2411-169: On the computational side, we see that even implementing the sub-problems sequentially leads to significant improvements over both Gurobi (that cannot exploit decomposability) or the classical Lagrangian dual.

elements.subsection-2411-012: Choice of bundle methods for various Lagrangian Dual
elements.sent-2411-371: In this subsection, we give some implementation details of the bundle method \cite{bagirov2014introduction we use to update multipliers in Lagrangian decomposition.}
elements.sent-2411-372: Among many variants of bundle methods, we use the proximal bundle method (without changing analytic center) with adaptive step size and the bundle-level method (without changing analytic center).
elements.sent-2411-373: We empirically observed that both the bundle methods have similar performance when applying to L.
elements.sent-2411-374: On the other hand, we also observed that the proximal bundle method has a better numerical performance when applied to QL, while the bundle-level method has a better numerical performance when applied to VL.
elements.sent-2411-375: Therefore, in the numerical experimental, we used the proximal bundle method for both L and QL and we used the bundle-level method for VL.
elements.sent-2411-376: For the proximal bundle method, we use adaptive step size motivated by the famous Polyak step size \cite{polyak2021introduction and recently numerically verified by \cite{monteiro2024parameter .}}
